questions.question: In the tool-calling architecture, what role does the host application play?
options: It trains the model to use tools correctly.
It decides whether to execute, modify, or reject every tool request the model makes.
It translates the model's natural-language output into structured API calls.
It provides the model with direct access to external systems.
questions.explanation: The host application is the gatekeeper. The model can only request actions — it never executes them directly. The host receives each request and applies its own validation, authorization, and business logic before deciding whether to proceed.

questions.question: Why is starting with a narrow set of exposed tools a recommended strategy?
options: Models perform worse when given access to many tools.
Each additional tool increases the potential blast radius if the model makes a poor decision, and widening access later is easier than narrowing it after an incident.
Tool definitions consume most of the context window, leaving little room for user input.
Regulatory compliance requires limiting AI systems to fewer than five tools.
questions.explanation: More tools mean more capability but also a wider scope of impact. Starting narrow follows the same principle of least privilege used in API and IAM design — you can always grant more access, but revoking it after a problem is harder.

questions.question: When a tool call fails, the model stops execution and requires a human operator to intervene before it can continue.
questions.explanation: When a tool call fails, the error information is returned to the model as text. The model can reason about the failure and decide on a next step — retrying with different parameters, trying an alternative tool, or explaining the situation to the user — without requiring human intervention.

questions.question: What is the primary architectural advantage of the model requesting actions rather than executing them directly?
options: It reduces latency by eliminating network round trips between the model and external systems.
It allows the model to learn from the results of each action and improve over time.
It creates a natural control and audit point where every AI-initiated action passes through application-owned validation, authorization, and logging.
It ensures the model's outputs are always deterministic and repeatable.
questions.explanation: Because every tool call passes through the host application, you get a built-in control point for security, permissions, and auditing. This is the fundamental trust model — the model proposes, the host disposes — and it is what makes deploying AI capabilities manageable from a governance perspective.
